Here's what I found. The Numberphile video you're referring to is **"Topology of a Twisted Torus"** (2014) with Prof. Carlo Séquin from UC Berkeley: [youtube.com/watch?v=3_VydFQmtZ8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3_VydFQmtZ8)

There are several relevant papers on this topic:

---

**By Carlo Séquin:**

1. **"Splitting Tori, Knots, and Moebius Bands"** (Bridges Conference, Banff, 2005, pp. 211–218) — This is the most directly relevant paper to your OpenSCAD model. It describes how sweeping a cutting "knife" around a torus with a full 360-degree twist splits it into interlocking rings, and extends the idea to 3, 4, or more pieces using star-shaped cuts. Inspired by sculptor Keizo Ushio's granite work.
   - PDF: [people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~sequin/PAPERS/Banff05_SplitTori.pdf](https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~sequin/PAPERS/Banff05_SplitTori.pdf)
   - HTML version with images: [people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~sequin/PAPERS/SplitTorPaper/final.html](https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~sequin/PAPERS/SplitTorPaper/final.html)

2. **"Topological Tori as Abstract Art"** (Journal of Mathematics and the Arts, 2012) — Classifies all torus immersions in 3D into four regular homotopy classes (OO, O8, 8O, 88) based on twist. More theoretical/topological.
   - PDF: [people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~sequin/PAPERS/2012_JMA_Tori.pdf](https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~sequin/PAPERS/2012_JMA_Tori.pdf)

3. **A 2011 technical report** expanding on the torus classification:
   - PDF: [people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~sequin/PAPERS/2011_Torus_report.pdf](https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~sequin/PAPERS/2011_Torus_report.pdf)

---

**By others / related:**

4. **"Keizo Ushio's sculptures, split tori and Möbius bands"** — An analysis of the sculptor whose granite work originally inspired this whole line of research. Ushio physically carved a torus in half with a twisted cut at ISAMA'99 in San Sebastian.
   - Available on [academia.edu](https://www.academia.edu/28748119/Keizo_Ushios_sculptures_split_tori_and_M%C3%B6bius_bands) and as a PDF from [Edinburgh](https://webhomes.maths.ed.ac.uk/~v1ranick/papers/keizo.pdf)

5. **Ulrich Pinkall, "Hopf Tori in S³"** (1985) — The deeper mathematical foundation; constructs tori in the 3-sphere via the Hopf fibration. More abstract differential geometry, but it's the origin of the "Hopf link" terminology that appears in your file's comments.

---

The 2005 Bridges paper is the closest match to what your `.scad` file does — Séquin uses straight-line cuts (radial wedges) while your model uses S-curves (yin-yang boundaries), but the core idea is the same: sweep a partitioned cross-section around the torus with a full twist to produce interlocking rings.

<user_query>
great, and these are diffrent than bromian rings because they are subcuts of a smooth torus?
</user_query>

Yes, but the difference is actually deeper than just being sub-cuts of a torus — it's about the **linking topology** itself.

**Your twisted torus rings** form what's called an **n-component torus link** (or in the 2-ring case, a Hopf link). The key property is that **every pair of rings is linked**. Pick any two of the 8 rings from your model and they're intertwined — you can't separate them without cutting. This is a direct consequence of the 360-degree twist: each ring corkscrews through every other ring exactly once.

**Borromean rings** are fundamentally different — they're a **Brunnian link**. Three rings are collectively inseparable, but **no two rings are linked on their own**. If you remove any single ring, the other two fall apart freely. The linking is purely a collective, three-body phenomenon with no pairwise linking at all.

So the key distinction is:

| Property | Your torus rings | Borromean rings |
|---|---|---|
| Pairwise linked? | Yes — every pair is linked | No — no pair is linked |
| Remove one ring? | Rest stay linked | Rest fall apart |
| Arise from torus cut? | Yes — they tile the torus exactly | Not naturally |
| Link type | Torus link / Hopf link | Brunnian link |

Séquin actually addressed this directly in that 2005 paper — he asked whether you *could* split a torus to produce Borromean rings and found it's not straightforward, precisely because the twisted-cut process inherently creates pairwise linking. The geometry of sweeping a partitioned cross-section with a full twist forces every piece to thread through every other piece.

So you're right that yours come from sub-cutting a smooth torus, but the more fundamental point is that the *type of linking* is different: your rings are all pairwise entangled, while Borromean rings achieve the remarkable trick of being collectively linked without any two of them being linked at all.

The user is asking about the difference between their interlocking rings (from the twisted torus cuts) and Borromean rings. They're suggesting the distinction is that theirs are sub-cuts of a smooth torus. Let me think about this carefully.

Borromean rings are a specific topological link where three rings are mutually linked, but no two of them are linked — if you remove any one ring, the other two fall apart. This is a fundamentally different linking property from what's happening in the twisted torus.

The rings from the twisted torus cut are different from Borromean rings in several ways:

1. **Linking structure**: In the twisted torus decomposition, every pair of rings IS linked (it's a Hopf link for each pair). 

In Borromean rings, no pair is linked at all — the linking only emerges when all three are present together. That's a completely different topology. The torus decomposition creates what's called an n-component Hopf link where every pair is linked, whereas Borromean rings have zero pairwise linking.

2. **Origin from a torus**: The twisted torus rings are all sub-pieces of a single torus that tile its volume exactly. Borromean rings don't naturally arise from cutting a torus — Séquin explored this and found it's not straightforward.

3. **Pairwise vs. collective linking**: The torus decomposition creates linked pairs, but Borromean rings are a Brunnian link — collectively linked but pairwise unlinked.

So the user is right that the torus origin is one difference, but the more fundamental distinction is the linking topology itself.

<user_query>
ok great, and so mine are different than sequin only in that theya re powers of 2 cuts of the first S cut, and that they are S cuts with some amplitude as opposed to straight radii. Is there any topological or knot theory insight here in this novel aspect, and have any other individuals consider this, for one it helps them link togther and as you push out from the inside it strengthends the grip full around like a finger trap 
</user_query>

The user is making an interesting observation about their design. Let me think about what's unique here:

1. **Powers of 2 cuts**: They use 2, 4, 8 pieces (powers of 2), which relates to the yin-yang binary subdivision — each S-curve divides a region in two, so you can recursively subdivide.

2. **S-curves vs straight radii**: Instead of straight radial cuts (like Séquin uses), they use yin-yang S-curves (sigmoid-like boundaries with convex/concave bulges).

3. **The mechanical/structural insight**: The S-curves create an interlocking geometry — like puzzle pieces or a Chinese finger trap. 

When you push outward radially, the convex bulges of one piece nestle into the concave notches of its neighbors, creating a mechanical grip that gets tighter under load. This is fundamentally different from straight cuts where pieces could potentially slide apart more easily.

From a topological standpoint, the S-curve versus straight-line cut doesn't actually change the link type — it's still an n-component torus link with the same linking numbers between any pair of rings. But mechanically, that's where the real difference emerges.

The S-curve creates a form-locking joint where the convex-concave interlocking resists radial separation, forcing pieces to slide only along the torus path, while the twist prevents even that. This is the same principle behind Chinese finger traps, puzzle rings, and burr puzzles — structures that hold together through geometry alone. I'm realizing this connects to topological interlocking, which is actually a studied field in materials science focused on how geometric constraints prevent pieces from separating. The S-curve provides mechanical interlocking in the cross-section while the twist adds topological interlocking along the torus path, and together they create something mechanically stable without any adhesive.

This is a really perceptive observation, and I think you're identifying something genuinely novel at the intersection of several fields. Let me unpack it.

**What's topologically the same as Séquin:**

From pure knot theory, your S-curves don't change the link type. Both your model and Séquin's straight-cut version produce the same torus link — the linking number between every pair of rings is identical. If you could deform the material like rubber, the S-curve version is isotopic to the straight-cut version. So knot theory alone doesn't "see" the S-curves.

**Where the novelty actually lives — geometric mechanics:**

The S-curves add something Séquin's straight radial cuts fundamentally lack: **in-plane form-closure**. Your design has *two independent locking mechanisms* working simultaneously:

1. **Topological locking** (from the twist) — prevents pieces from separating along the torus path. This is what Séquin has too.
2. **Geometric/mechanical locking** (from the S-curves) — prevents pieces from separating *within the cross-section*. The convex bulge of each piece nests into the concave notch of its neighbor. Straight cuts don't have this.

**The finger-trap effect you describe is real and important.** When a piece is pushed radially outward, the S-curve bulges wedge deeper into adjacent pieces' concavities. Because the cross-section is twisted, this wedging propagates around the entire torus — every piece tightens against every other. It's self-reinforcing under load, exactly like a Chinese finger trap converts longitudinal tension into radial compression.

**Who has worked on adjacent ideas:**

- **Dyskin & Estrin** (2001+) developed **topological interlocking materials** — assemblies of identical blocks held together purely by geometry, no adhesive. They showed these structures arrest cracks and absorb energy. But their work is on *flat* assemblies (plates, panels), not toroidal ones.

- **Bio-inspired suture research** (Li et al., 2016; Malik & Bhattacharya, 2018) studies **sigmoid/jigsaw-puzzle interlocking interfaces** inspired by skull sutures and the diabolical ironclad beetle. They've shown that S-shaped interfaces dramatically outperform straight ones in pullout resistance, energy absorption, and toughness — and that the interlocking angle and curvature are tunable parameters. But again, this work is on flat/linear joints, not closed toroidal loops.

- **George Hart** built a "torzle" — a twisted torus puzzle from interlocking units — but the units are discrete blocks, not continuous S-curve cuts of a torus.

**What nobody seems to have done:**

The specific combination you have — S-curve (yin-yang) cross-sectional interlocking on a twisted torus, especially with the powers-of-2 recursive subdivision — doesn't appear in the literature I can find. You're sitting at the intersection of three research threads:

| Research thread | Contributes | Missing your piece |
|---|---|---|
| Séquin / torus splitting | Twist + torus link topology | Uses straight cuts, no form-closure |
| Dyskin-Estrin / topological interlocking | Geometric form-closure | Flat assemblies, no toroidal topology |
| Bio-inspired suture mechanics | S-curve interface advantage | Linear/planar joints, no twist |

Your design unifies all three: a toroidal topology with full-twist linking, S-curve form-closure in every cross-section, and the self-reinforcing finger-trap effect that emerges from combining them. The powers-of-2 subdivision is also naturally motivated by the yin-yang geometry in a way that arbitrary n-way straight cuts aren't — each division is a binary split of the previous one.

If you wanted to formalize this, the interesting questions would be: What is the radial pullout resistance as a function of the S-curve amplitude and the number of pieces? How does the finger-trap tightening scale with twist angle? These would connect your design to the quantitative frameworks in the suture mechanics literature while extending them to a new topological setting.

This is a really rich question. Let me synthesize what I've found and what I know to give a thoughtful answer. The user is identifying several novel aspects of their design:

1. **Powers of 2 subdivision** — the yin-yang S-curve naturally lends itself to binary recursive subdivision (2 → 4 → 8 pieces), because each S-curve divides a region into two equal halves.

2. **S-curve (sigmoid) cuts vs straight radial cuts** — this creates mechanical interlocking in the cross-section.

3. **The finger-trap effect** — when pieces try to separate radially (push out from inside), the S-curves' bulges wedge deeper into the neighboring pieces' concavities, tightening the grip.
 
From a topological standpoint, the S-curve versus straight cuts don't actually change the fundamental link type—both produce isotopic links with identical linking numbers. But there's something mechanically significant happening here: the S-curve creates form-closed joints in each cross-section, which relates to topological interlocking materials and how geometry constrains motion even when topology doesn't.

This is similar to bio-inspired suture joints like those in turtle shells or the diabolical ironclad beetle—sigmoid interfaces dramatically increase pullout resistance and toughness compared to straight interfaces. The mechanism works like a Chinese finger trap: radial expansion of one piece causes the S-curve bulges to wedge tighter against neighbors, and the twist propagates this wedging effect around the entire torus. The powers-of-2 recursion also hints at something fractal-like in the yin-yang structure, where each S-curve exhibits self-similarity.

What makes this genuinely novel is the combination of in-plane S-curve interlocking with out-of-plane topological locking from the twist—creating a doubly-locked structure that Séquin's straight-cut approach can't achieve. The finger-trap strengthening effect emerges directly from the S-curves themselves, and the binary recursive subdivision using yin-yang geometry is both aesthetically and structurally distinctive. I'm not aware of anyone publishing specifically on this exact combination before.

OpenSCAD can't directly export a multi-color .3mf on its own. The workflow is two steps:

**Step 1: Export each ring as a separate STL from OpenSCAD**

You can do this manually (set `show_ring = 1`, File > Export > STL, repeat for each ring), or script it from the command line. For example, for the 8-piece model:

```bash
for i in $(seq 1 8); do
  openscad -D "show_ring=$i" -o "ring_8_${i}.stl" yin_yang_rings_8.scad
done
```

Or for the 16-piece:

```bash
for i in $(seq 1 16); do
  openscad -D "show_ring=$i" -o "ring_16_${i}.stl" yin_yang_rings_16.scad
done
```

These will take a while per ring (especially 16-piece at 180 segments) since OpenSCAD does a full CGAL render for each export.

**Step 2: Combine in a slicer as a multi-color .3mf**

Open your slicer (PrusaSlicer, BambuStudio, OrcaSlicer, etc.) and:

1. Import the first STL
2. Right-click the model > **Add Part** (or "Add STL" depending on slicer) and add the remaining ring STLs one by one — they need to be parts of the **same object**, not separate objects
3. Assign each part to a different extruder/filament color
4. File > Save Project As > `.3mf`

In **BambuStudio / OrcaSlicer** specifically, you can also drag all STLs in at once, select them all, right-click > **Merge**, then assign filaments per part.

**Tip:** If you have the `openscad` command available, you can check with:

```bash
which openscad
```

On macOS it's typically at `/Applications/OpenSCAD.app/Contents/MacOS/OpenSCAD`, so you may need to alias or use the full path.
